messages.content: Diana Cho's deposition was yesterday. Six hours. I'm exhausted but there's a lot to document.
Six hours of a prepared corporate witness is exhausting work — take a moment, then let's capture everything carefully while it's fresh.

Diana was extremely well-prepared. Corporate-trained witness. Every answer was precise, she never volunteered anything, and she consistently redirected to the narrative that Marcus's termination was purely a performance and restructuring decision.

The corporate preparation is expected for a CEO-level defendant. Precise, non-volunteering answers are a mark of good witness coaching rather than necessarily truthfulness — the value in deposing a well-coached witness is often not in what they say but in what they commit to, which creates the framework for impeachment later. What specific commitments did she make that are useful to us?

The key moment — I asked Diana directly: "Did Marcus Webb ever express concerns to you about the financial projections prepared for the Series C fundraise?" She answered: "Marcus and I had conversations about many aspects of the business. I don't recall specific concerns about projections."

Logging this verbatim as it's strategically significant. Diana's answer: "I don't recall specific concerns about projections." I'm immediately cross-referencing this against what's already in the record. We have a calendar invite in discovery for a December 2022 meeting titled "Series C Readiness — Exec Alignment" that includes Diana, Richard, Marcus, and others. We have James Whitfield's statement that Diana was present in at least one meeting where the projection dispute was discussed. Diana's "no recollection" answer is now directly contradicted by two separate sources. The calendar record is particularly powerful — it's a contemporaneous system record, not a witness's memory.

Diana also testified that the January 2023 one-on-one with Marcus was a "routine check-in" and not performance-related. But Priya testified it was related to "role clarity" which has different implications. And Marcus says Diana mentioned "board concerns about leadership style."

I need to flag a three-way conflict here and I want to document it precisely because it may be one of the most important inconsistencies in the case. The January 2023 Diana-Marcus meeting has now been characterized three different ways by three different people. Diana Cho (deposition, Month 9): "routine check-in," not performance-related. Priya Anand (deposition, Month 2): meeting related to "role clarity." Marcus Webb (follow-up call, Month 5, after initially not remembering the meeting): Diana said "the board was concerned about his leadership style." These three characterizations are materially different. A "routine check-in" is innocuous. "Role clarity" suggests organizational concern. "Board concerns about leadership style" suggests substantive performance feedback six weeks before termination. Vertex will try to use any version of this meeting that supports a performance management narrative. We need to be ready to address all three versions. Do you have any documentary evidence of what the meeting actually covered?

One new development — Vertex's counsel produced a document in this week's supplemental discovery that we hadn't seen before. It's a performance improvement plan supposedly drafted for Marcus in December 2022. Marcus says he never saw this document and was never put on a PIP. Vertex claims it was "in preparation" and never formally delivered.

This is a significant development and I want to flag it with appropriate urgency. A performance improvement plan allegedly drafted in December 2022 that Marcus has never seen and was never delivered — appearing for the first time in supplemental discovery nine months into litigation — raises immediate authenticity concerns. The "in preparation, never delivered" explanation is legally convenient but factually suspicious: PIPs are routinely documented and communicated as part of employment law compliance, not drafted and filed without delivery. Most critically: if this document was actually created after the termination rather than in December 2022, it is potentially fabricated evidence. The metadata examination you mentioned is not optional — it is urgent. Document creation date, last modified date, author properties, version history — all of this needs a forensic expert immediately.

This PIP document is suspicious to me. The metadata might tell us something about when it was actually created. I want to get a forensic expert to look at the document metadata. If that PIP was created after the termination it's potentially fabricated evidence and completely changes the character of this case.

Agreed on all counts — and I want to make sure the record captures the full implication. If metadata shows creation after February 14, 2023 (the termination date), this document is potentially fabricated evidence introduced in federal litigation. That's no longer just a wrongful termination case — it's potential obstruction and fraud on the court. The case posture would shift dramatically: sanctions, adverse inference instructions, potential referral. Even if it doesn't rise to that level, a post-termination creation date fundamentally destroys Vertex's performance management narrative. Get the forensic expert engaged immediately. Preserve the document as produced, including all metadata, and do not request a "native" copy through normal channels — that could trigger a new production that replaces the original. Do you have the document in its original produced format?

I also want to revisit whether we should add a claim for fraudulent misrepresentation given what's emerging about the Series C projections. That potentially opens up individual liability for Richard and Diana beyond Vertex as a company.

This is a significant strategic consideration and the timing matters. Adding a claim mid-litigation requires amendment, which the court may permit depending on your scheduling order. The fraudulent misrepresentation theory — that Diana and Richard made material misrepresentations in the Series C projections — potentially creates individual liability that corporate Vertex cannot indemnify, which changes the settlement calculus significantly. The strength of the claim depends on what the projections actually said versus what the internal communications show they knew, and whether there was actual reliance by third-party investors. Before amending, I'd want to understand: what happened with the Series C? Did it close? Were investors harmed? The answers to those questions determine whether there's a viable fraud claim or just evidence of internal pressure. I've flagged this as an open strategic question: potential fraud claim against Cho and Okafor individually, evaluation pending.
